about.projectDescription: TARINIO is a protection and traffic control layer that intercepts incoming web requests, analyzes and inspects them using WAF/CRS rules, applies limits and anti-DDoS measures (L4/L7), and centrally manages changes through revisions (compile/validate/apply/rollback), blocking attacks and unwanted traffic before it reaches the protected system.

Collect runtime host/container hardening evidence: tcp_timestamps, sysctl and TLS/HSTS directives.

antiddos.help.modelDecay: Controls how quickly old suspicious behavior loses score. Increase to forgive clients faster; decrease for longer memory.
antiddos.help.modelDropThreshold: Score level for hard mitigation (drop). Keep above throttle threshold to avoid premature hard bans.
antiddos.help.modelEmergencyPerIpRps: Per-IP requests per second for single-source emergency signal. Lower for aggressive single host floods.
antiddos.help.modelEmergencyRps: Total requests per second that marks emergency surge. Lower for earlier emergency reaction.
antiddos.help.modelEmergencyUniqueIps: Unique clients per second for botnet-like emergency signal. Lower captures distributed floods faster.
antiddos.help.modelEnabled: Turns the model on or off. Keep enabled in production; disable only for isolated diagnostics.
antiddos.help.modelHoldSeconds: How long the mitigation record stays active after a trigger. Increase for persistent attacks, decrease for test environments.
antiddos.help.modelPoll: How often model reads fresh access logs. Lower value reacts faster but increases CPU usage.
antiddos.help.modelThrottleBurst: Temporary burst allowance during throttle stage. Increase if real users do short bursts and get challenged too often.
antiddos.help.modelThrottleRps: Per-IP RPS cap during throttle stage. Lower means stronger shaping for suspicious clients.
antiddos.help.modelThrottleTarget: Packet action while throttling. REJECT is user-visible and friendlier; DROP is stricter and stealthier.
antiddos.help.modelThrottleThreshold: Score level to start soft mitigation (throttle). Lower value is stricter for burst traffic.
antiddos.help.modelWeight403: Score contribution per HTTP 403. Raise when blocked requests should escalate faster.
antiddos.help.modelWeight429: Score contribution per HTTP 429. Raise if rate-limit bypass attempts are too frequent.
antiddos.help.modelWeight444: Score contribution per NGINX 444 events. Usually strongest signal of malicious probing.
antiddos.help.modelWeightEmergencyBotnet: Score added to every participant during distributed emergency. Raise for faster drop escalation under botnet floods.
antiddos.help.modelWeightEmergencySingle: Score added to source IP during single-source emergency. Raise if one attacker still reaches backend under throttle.
antiddos.help.subtitle: Purpose, default value and safe tuning direction for each parameter.
antiddos.help.title: Adaptive model parameter guide

administration.enterprise.approvals.subt: Gate revision apply with a configurable multi-approver policy.
administration.enterprise.scim.title: SCIM provisioning
administration.enterprise.scim.subtitle: Issue bearer tokens for user provisioning and map external groups to local roles.
sites.easy.traffic.frame.badBehavior.sub: Auto-ban based on suspicious response codes
sites.easy.traffic.frame.limits.subtitle: Connection and request rate limits
sites.easy.traffic.frame.dnsbl.subtitle: Access lists, DNSBL and block lists
sites.easy.traffic.badBehavior.help.subt: How the WAF tracks suspicious responses and automatically bans sources.
sites.easy.traffic.badBehavior.help.acti: Enables bad-behaviour monitoring for this service. Without it the limits below do not apply.
sites.easy.traffic.badBehavior.help.stat: Which response codes are treated as bad behaviour and counted.
sites.easy.traffic.badBehavior.help.banD: Base ban duration in seconds. 0 means a permanent ban.
sites.easy.traffic.badBehavior.help.thre: How many suspicious requests from one source are allowed within the period.
sites.easy.traffic.badBehavior.help.peri: Counting window in seconds. After it expires the counter resets.
sites.easy.traffic.limits.help.title: Limits help
sites.easy.traffic.limits.help.subtitle: Connection and request-rate limits for this service.
sites.easy.traffic.limits.help.connEnabl: Enables limits on the number of concurrent connections per client.
sites.easy.traffic.limits.help.maxHttp1: Maximum concurrent HTTP/1.x connections per source IP.
sites.easy.traffic.limits.help.maxHttp2: Maximum concurrent HTTP/2 streams per source IP.
sites.easy.traffic.limits.help.maxHttp3: Maximum concurrent HTTP/3 streams per source IP.
sites.easy.traffic.limits.help.reqEnable: Enables request rate limiting. Without it the fields below are inactive.
sites.easy.traffic.limits.help.reqUrl: URL or prefix to which the global rate limit applies.
sites.easy.traffic.limits.help.custom: Per-path overrides with their own rate.
sites.easy.traffic.dnsbl.help.title: Lists and DNSBL help
sites.easy.traffic.dnsbl.help.subtitle: Access lists, DNSBL and black lists of various types.
sites.easy.traffic.dnsbl.help.blacklist: Enables blacklists for IP, ASN, rDNS, User-Agent and URI.
sites.easy.traffic.dnsbl.help.dnsbl: Enables external DNSBL checks for client IPs.
sites.easy.traffic.dnsbl.help.allowlist: Enables the allow-list. When populated, only listed entries are allowed.
sites.easy.traffic.dnsbl.help.exceptions: Enables an IP exceptions list that bypasses all other blocks.
sites.easy.traffic.dnsbl.help.lists: Denylist, Allowlist and Exceptions control access manually.
sites.easy.traffic.dnsbl.help.blacklistB: Blacklists by IP, rDNS, ASN, User-Agent and URI apply alongside DNSBL.
sites.easy.traffic.dnsbl.help.sources: URLs that regularly update lists for each type.
sites.easy.upstream.headers.help.title: Header forwarding help
sites.easy.upstream.headers.help.subtitl: Which headers the WAF adds or preserves when proxying to the upstream.
sites.easy.upstream.headers.help.passHos: Forward the original client Host instead of substituting reverse_proxy_host.
sites.easy.upstream.headers.help.xff: Append X-Forwarded-For with client IP and proxy chain.
sites.easy.upstream.headers.help.xfp: Append X-Forwarded-Proto with the client's original scheme (http or https).
sites.easy.upstream.headers.help.xri: Append X-Real-IP with the actual client address.
sites.easy.antibot.help.title: Anti-bot help
sites.easy.antibot.help.subtitle: How challenge modes, exclusions and escalation affect real traffic.
sites.easy.auth.help.title: Authentication help
sites.easy.auth.help.subtitle: How to safely combine interactive login, service tokens and path exclusions.
sites.easy.auth.exclusionRulesHint: These paths bypass the login page. Useful for API and webhooks.
sites.easy.auth.exclusionMethodsHelp: Specify a comma-separated list of HTTP methods or * for all methods.
sites.easy.auth.serviceTokensHint: Static tokens for backend integrations. Treat them as secrets.
sites.validation.authExclusionRulesLimit: Auth exclusion rules must not exceed 32 entries
sites.validation.authExclusionPathFormat: Auth exclusion path must start with /
sites.validation.authExclusionMethodsInv: Auth exclusion methods must be valid HTTP methods or a single *
sites.validation.authServiceTokenRequire: Each service token must have a name
sites.validation.authServiceTokenSecretR: Active service tokens must have a secret
sites.help.front.title: Front service: help
sites.help.front.subtitle: What each field means and what to put in on the front-end page.
sites.help.front.serverName.label: Hostname
sites.help.front.serverName.usage: Public domain the service answers on. Put the exact domain users will type, for example app.example.com. The domain must already point to this server so the WAF can validate it and issue certificates.
sites.help.front.serviceId.label: Service identifier
sites.help.front.serviceId.usage: Short internal name used in configs and logs. Latin lowercase with dashes, for example shop-prod. Leave empty to derive it from the hostname automatically.
sites.help.front.securityMode.label: Security mode
sites.help.front.securityMode.usage: Controls what the WAF does with suspicious requests. transparent passes everything and is only for debugging. monitor logs hits without blocking and is great for rule trialling. block actually blocks classified attacks and is the production default.
sites.help.front.serviceProfile.label: Service profile
sites.help.front.serviceProfile.usage: Preset of rules tuned for a site type. strict for plain static sites, balanced for the common case, compat for legacy apps, api for REST and microservices, public-edge for high-traffic public portals. You can still adjust individual settings afterwards.
sites.help.front.caServer.label: Certificate authority
sites.help.front.caServer.usage: Where TLS certificates come from. letsencrypt and zerossl issue free certificates automatically. custom points to your own ACME server. import lets you upload an existing certificate manually.
sites.help.front.serviceEnabled.label: Service enabled
sites.help.front.serviceEnabled.usage: Master switch for the service. With the flag off the WAF stops accepting traffic for this domain. Useful for a temporary outage without losing the configuration.
sites.help.front.adaptiveModel.label: Adaptive model
sites.help.front.adaptiveModel.usage: Enables the behavioural model that learns from real traffic and tightens defences on anomalies. Best for sites with steady, recurring audiences.
sites.help.front.autoLetsEncrypt.label: Auto Lets Encrypt
sites.help.front.autoLetsEncrypt.usage: Order and renew Lets Encrypt certificates automatically. The domain must resolve to this server and ports 80 and 443 must be reachable from the public internet.
sites.help.front.letsEncryptStaging.usag: Use the Lets Encrypt staging endpoint. Certificates will not be browser-trusted but you avoid hitting production issuance rate limits. Turn off before going live.
sites.help.front.wildcard.label: Wildcard certificate
sites.help.front.wildcard.usage: Order a wildcard certificate covering all subdomains. Requires DNS-01 support from the chosen authority and DNS control over the zone.
sites.help.front.tlsEnabled.label: TLS enabled
sites.help.front.tlsEnabled.usage: Accept HTTPS on this service. With the flag off the domain runs over plain HTTP. Always leave on in production.
sites.help.front.tlsSelfSigned.label: Self-signed TLS
sites.help.front.tlsSelfSigned.usage: Use a self-signed certificate. Browsers will show a warning. Only suitable for internal staging environments and debugging.
sites.help.front.certificateId.label: Certificate identifier
sites.help.front.certificateId.usage: Reference to a certificate that is already uploaded to the WAF. Only relevant when the authority is set to import; otherwise the value is filled in automatically.
sites.help.upstream.title: Upstream: help
sites.help.upstream.subtitle: Where the WAF forwards traffic to and which headers it carries through.
sites.help.upstream.useReverseProxy.usag: When on, the WAF acts as a reverse proxy and forwards requests to your application using the fields below. When off, the WAF only serves static content or a placeholder.
sites.help.upstream.keepalive.label: Upstream keep-alive
sites.help.upstream.keepalive.usage: Hold persistent connections to your application instead of opening a new one per request. Lowers latency and load; keep it on if the application supports HTTP keep-alive.
sites.help.upstream.websocket.label: WebSocket support
sites.help.upstream.websocket.usage: Allow upgrading the connection to WebSocket and proxy it to the application. Enable for chats, live feeds, server push, and any duplex protocol on top of HTTP.
sites.help.upstream.customHost.label: Custom Host header
sites.help.upstream.customHost.usage: If set, WAF replaces the Host header sent to upstream with this value instead of the public site domain. Only effective when the pass Host flag is enabled. Use when the application expects a fixed internal hostname (e.g. internal.app.local) different from the public domain. Leave empty to forward the original client Host.
sites.help.upstream.reverseProxyHost.usa: Hostname the WAF puts into the Host header when calling the upstream. Use it when your application expects a fixed internal domain rather than the public site domain.
sites.help.upstream.reverseProxyUrl.usag: Full base URL the WAF forwards requests to, for example https://internal.app.local. When set, scheme and port come from this URL and override the fields below.
sites.help.upstream.target.label: Target: scheme, host, port
sites.help.upstream.target.usage: Three fields define the upstream address. Scheme is http or https depending on how the application accepts traffic internally. Host is the DNS name or IP. Port is where the application listens, for example 8080.
sites.help.upstream.headerForwarding.usa: A nested frame with its own question-mark help. Controls forwarding of Host, X-Forwarded-For, X-Forwarded-Proto and X-Real-IP so the upstream sees the real client IP and scheme.
sites.help.upstream.sslSni.label: TLS SNI to upstream
sites.help.upstream.sslSni.usage: Send a TLS SNI hostname when speaking HTTPS to the upstream. Needed when the upstream serves multiple TLS sites on the same IP and decides which certificate to present based on SNI.
sites.help.http.title: Generic HTTP: help
sites.help.http.subtitle: Protocol-level basics: methods, body size and supported HTTP versions.
sites.help.http.allowedMethods.label: Allowed HTTP methods
sites.help.http.allowedMethods.usage: Allowlist of HTTP methods the service accepts at all. Anything not listed is rejected immediately. A website usually needs GET, HEAD, POST. APIs add PUT, PATCH, DELETE, OPTIONS. TRACE and CONNECT are almost never required and should be left out.
sites.help.http.maxBodySize.label: Maximum body size
sites.help.http.maxBodySize.usage: Cap on request body size. Use suffix form like 1m, 10m, 100k. Anything larger is rejected with HTTP 413. Helps against large data dumps. Account for legitimate file uploads when picking the value.
sites.help.http.sslProtocols.label: TLS versions
sites.help.http.sslProtocols.usage: Which TLS versions to accept. A safe modern set is TLSv1.3 and TLSv1.2. TLSv1.0 and TLSv1.1 are deprecated and should stay disabled unless you have a very old client that cannot be upgraded.
sites.help.http.http2.label: HTTP/2 support
sites.help.http.http2.usage: Accept HTTP/2 connections. Reduces latency and connection count. Enable unless an older load balancer in front of the WAF cannot speak HTTP/2.
sites.help.http.http3.label: HTTP/3 support
sites.help.http.http3.usage: Accept HTTP/3 over QUIC. Gives faster connection setup and better loss tolerance. Requires open UDP ports for QUIC and modern clients.
sites.help.http.strictParsing.label: Strict HTTP parsing
sites.help.http.strictParsing.usage: Enables strict HTTP request validation: disallows headers with underscores, ignores invalid headers, and clears Transfer-Encoding before proxying. Protects against HTTP Request Smuggling / Desync attacks. Recommended for standard HTTP/1.1 upstreams. Caution: some clients send non-standard headers — verify traffic before enabling in production.
sites.help.headers.title: HTTP headers: help
sites.help.headers.subtitle: Cookie flags, CORS, HSTS, CSP and other secure response policies.
sites.help.headers.cookieFlags.label: Cookie flags
sites.help.headers.cookieFlags.usage: Base flags appended to Set-Cookie. Typical entries: Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite. Secure refuses to send the cookie over HTTP, HttpOnly hides it from JavaScript, SameSite restricts cross-site sends. These three together are the standard baseline for session cookies.
sites.help.headers.referrerPolicy.label: Referrer-Policy
sites.help.headers.referrerPolicy.usage: How much information about the origin is revealed when a user follows a link. Safe values are no-referrer, strict-origin, or strict-origin-when-cross-origin. Leaky values may expose tokens carried in URLs.
sites.help.headers.hsts.label: HSTS enabled
sites.help.headers.hsts.usage: Forces browsers to always reach the site over HTTPS, even if the user typed http://. Enable once HTTPS is stable; rolling back to HTTP afterwards is hard.
sites.help.headers.hstsMaxAge.label: HSTS max-age (seconds)
sites.help.headers.hstsMaxAge.usage: How long the browser remembers the HTTPS-only rule. The common value is 31536000 seconds, which is one year. Use shorter values only during rollouts.
sites.help.headers.hstsIncludeSubdomains: Extends the HTTPS-only rule to every subdomain. Enable only when every subdomain is ready to serve HTTPS.
sites.help.headers.hstsPreload.label: HSTS preload
sites.help.headers.hstsPreload.usage: Marks the domain as ready for the browser preload list. Once registered, fresh users cannot reach the site over HTTP at all. Only enable when all subdomains are HTTPS-ready.
sites.help.headers.csp.label: Content-Security-Policy
sites.help.headers.csp.usage: The main header that restricts where the page can load scripts, styles, images and fonts from. Big mitigation for XSS. A good starting policy is script-src self and gradually broaden it for needed origins.
sites.help.headers.permissionsPolicy.usa: List of browser features the page is allowed to use. Entries like geolocation=(), camera=(), microphone=() disable those APIs entirely so scripts cannot request access.
sites.help.headers.keepUpstreamHeaders.u: Which response headers from the application are forwarded to the client as-is, without rewriting. Add things like X-Request-Id or your custom diagnostic headers here. Security headers are always added by the WAF on top.
sites.help.headers.useCors.label: CORS enabled
sites.help.headers.useCors.usage: Allow cross-origin requests to this service. Needed when the front-end and the API live on different domains. Without CORS the browser blocks such requests.
sites.help.headers.corsAllowedOrigins.us: Explicit list of origins allowed to call this service across domains. One entry per line, for example https://app.example.com. Avoid using * because it allows everyone.
sites.help.blocking.title: Blocking: help
sites.help.blocking.subtitle: Automatic ban escalation stages and their scope.
sites.help.blocking.enabled.label: Escalation enabled
sites.help.blocking.enabled.usage: When on, the ban duration grows with repeated offences. For example first offence five minutes, second one hour, third permanent. Escalation runs on top of regular bans and cuts off persistent attackers fast.
sites.help.blocking.scope.label: Scope
sites.help.blocking.scope.usage: Where the offence counter is stored. current_site keeps offences inside this service. all_sites pools offences across every service, useful when the same bots hit your whole estate.
sites.help.blocking.stages.label: Ban stages
sites.help.blocking.stages.usage: Ordered list of ban durations from soft to hard. Each repeat moves to the next stage. A stage of 0 is a permanent ban and must be the last item.
sites.help.blocking.stageFormat.label: Value format
sites.help.blocking.stageFormat.usage: Use compact duration syntax: 30s, 5m, 1h, 24h. The value 0 stands for a permanent ban. Negative values are not allowed.
sites.help.antibot.title: Anti-bot and authentication: help
sites.help.antibot.subtitle: Chapter overview. Two inner frames have their own detailed help.
sites.help.antibot.frameAntibot.label: Anti-bot frame
sites.help.antibot.frameAntibot.usage: Configures the bot challenge itself: challenge type, captcha provider, the challenge URL, path exclusions, and the second-layer escalation. The frame has its own question-mark help with detailed methodology.
sites.help.antibot.frameAuth.label: Authentication frame
sites.help.antibot.frameAuth.usage: Turns on the login wall: HTTP Basic, service tokens, or both. The frame has its own question-mark help describing every field and recommended secret-handling practice.
sites.help.antibot.order.label: Protection order
sites.help.antibot.order.usage: Which protection runs first on a request: bot check or authentication. Anti-bot first is safer against brute forcing because bots never reach the login form. Authentication first feels nicer for trusted, closed services because users see the login right away.
sites.help.antibot.exclusions.label: Path exclusions
sites.help.antibot.exclusions.usage: Each frame ships its own list of paths and methods that bypass the corresponding check. Use it for server-to-server integrations, webhooks, and health-check endpoints.
sites.help.antibot.escalation.label: Two-layer escalation
sites.help.antibot.escalation.usage: Option inside the anti-bot frame. After the first challenge passes you can require a second, stricter challenge on sensitive pages (for example a cookie-challenge followed by captcha). Use it on login and admin areas.
sites.help.geo.title: Country policy: help
sites.help.geo.subtitle: Black and white lists of countries and country groups.
sites.help.geo.showGeoBlockPage.label: Region restriction page
sites.help.geo.showGeoBlockPage.usage: When enabled, geo-blocked requests receive a 451 status and see an informational page explaining the restriction with suggested steps (disable VPN, switch network). The page auto-detects the browser language (English, Russian, German).
sites.help.geo.blacklist.label: Blocked countries
sites.help.geo.blacklist.usage: Countries whose traffic is blocked outright before any other checks. Use it to silence regions you definitely do not serve and that only produce noise.
sites.help.geo.whitelist.label: Allowed countries
sites.help.geo.whitelist.usage: When at least one country is listed, only those countries are accepted; everyone else is blocked. Use it for local-only businesses.
sites.help.geo.groups.label: Country groups
sites.help.geo.groups.usage: Add ready-made groups instead of one country at a time, for example continents, CIS countries, or G20. The WAF expands the group into a country list at compile time.
sites.help.geo.timeWindows.label: Time windows
sites.help.geo.timeWindows.usage: Block or allow traffic from specific countries only during certain hours and days of the week (UTC). For example, block Russia and China from 9 to 17 on weekdays. Empty windows are ignored.
sites.help.modsec.title: ModSecurity: help
sites.help.modsec.subtitle: OWASP CRS, plugins and your own custom rules.
sites.help.modsec.useModsec.label: ModSecurity enabled
sites.help.modsec.useModsec.usage: Master switch for the ModSecurity engine on this service. With the flag off no CRS rules apply. Keep it on unless you are actively debugging a false positive.
sites.help.modsec.useCrsPlugins.label: Use CRS plugins
sites.help.modsec.useCrsPlugins.usage: Enables OWASP Core Rule Set plugins. Plugins add targeted rules for popular stacks like WordPress or PHP and cut down false positives on those platforms.
sites.help.modsec.useCustom.label: Use custom rules
sites.help.modsec.useCustom.usage: Enables your own custom rule block. The rules live in the fields below. Turn it on when stock CRS is not enough and you need a specific filter for your application.
sites.help.modsec.crsVersion.label: CRS version
sites.help.modsec.crsVersion.usage: Which OWASP CRS version to load. Newer is wider in coverage but may flag new false positives. Leave empty to take the default bundled version.
sites.help.modsec.plugins.label: Plugin list
sites.help.modsec.plugins.usage: Identifiers of CRS plugins to load, one per entry, for example wordpress-rule-exclusions. See the OWASP CRS docs for available plugins.
sites.help.modsec.customPath.label: Custom config path
sites.help.modsec.customPath.usage: Path inside the WAF container to a file with your custom ModSecurity rules. If you do not have a separate file, leave the default and write the rules in the next field.
sites.help.modsec.customContent.label: Custom rules content
sites.help.modsec.customContent.usage: Your ModSecurity rules in SecRule form. Only honored when the custom rules flag is on. Syntax errors here break configuration apply for the whole service, so be careful.
sites.help.traffic.badBehavior.title: Bad behaviour: help
sites.help.traffic.badBehavior.subtitle: Automatic ban for clients that keep getting suspicious response codes.
Master switch for the mechanism. With the flag off the fields below are ignored and no automatic ban happens. Enable so the WAF punishes sources of suspicious traffic on its own.
sites.help.traffic.badBehavior.statusCod: HTTP codes counted as suspicious. Typical entries are 401, 403, 404, 444 and 429. A client that keeps receiving these is most likely scanning or brute-forcing.
sites.help.traffic.badBehavior.banDurati: How many seconds the offender stays banned once the threshold trips. For example 600 means ten minutes. A value of 0 means a permanent ban.
sites.help.traffic.badBehavior.threshold: How many suspicious requests one source may issue within the period below. For instance 20 means twenty suspicious responses in a row send the source to a ban. Tighter services use lower numbers.
sites.help.traffic.badBehavior.period.us: Sliding window the offences are counted in. For example 60 keeps a one-minute history. After the window expires the counter resets and the behaviour is forgiven.
sites.help.traffic.limits.title: Limits: help
sites.help.traffic.limits.subtitle: Connection-count and request-rate limits.
sites.help.traffic.limits.connEnable.usa: Turns on the limit on concurrent connections per client. Helps against simple slot exhaustion and slowloris-style attacks.
sites.help.traffic.limits.maxHttp1.label: Max HTTP/1.x connections
sites.help.traffic.limits.maxHttp1.usage: Concurrent HTTP/1.0 and 1.1 connections allowed from one IP. Usually 10 to 50 is enough. Very low values may break multi-tab usage of the web UI.
sites.help.traffic.limits.maxHttp2.label: Max HTTP/2 streams
sites.help.traffic.limits.maxHttp2.usage: Concurrent HTTP/2 streams allowed on one connection. Browsers easily open several dozen. Lower it when you see DDoS via stream multiplexing.
sites.help.traffic.limits.maxHttp3.label: Max HTTP/3 streams
sites.help.traffic.limits.maxHttp3.usage: Equivalent of HTTP/2 streams but over QUIC. Use only when HTTP/3 is enabled in the generic HTTP settings.
sites.help.traffic.limits.reqEnable.usag: Turns on the service-wide request rate limit. With the flag off the URL and rate fields have no effect.
sites.help.traffic.limits.reqUrl.label: Limit URL
sites.help.traffic.limits.reqUrl.usage: Path prefix where the global rate limit applies. An empty value or a single / applies to the whole service. To limit only /api put /api here.
sites.help.traffic.limits.reqRate.label: Rate
sites.help.traffic.limits.reqRate.usage: Average requests per second or per minute allowed. Format: 20r/s for twenty per second, 100r/m for one hundred per minute.
sites.help.traffic.limits.custom.label: Per-path limits
sites.help.traffic.limits.custom.usage: Pairs of path and rate. For example /login at 5r/m protects the login form from brute force. Applied on top of the global limit.
sites.help.traffic.dnsbl.title: Lists and DNSBL: help
sites.help.traffic.dnsbl.subtitle: Allow lists, deny lists and DNSBL configuration.
sites.help.traffic.dnsbl.blacklistToggle: Enables every type of blacklist below at once: IP, ASN, reverse DNS, user-agent, URI. With the flag off lists stay stored but are not applied.
sites.help.traffic.dnsbl.dnsblToggle.usa: Checks each client IP against external DNSBL services such as Spamhaus. Slows down the first request from a fresh IP but catches well-known botnets and spam networks.
sites.help.traffic.dnsbl.allowlistToggle: When on and the allowlist has at least one entry, only those addresses are accepted. Everyone else is blocked. Use it for private environments.
sites.help.traffic.dnsbl.exceptionsToggl: Enables a list of trusted IPs that bypass every other block and limit. Use for office, monitoring, and trusted API partners.
sites.help.traffic.dnsbl.lists.label: Access lists
sites.help.traffic.dnsbl.lists.usage: Three manual lists: denylist for targeted blocks, allowlist for targeted approvals, exceptions for full passes. One IP or CIDR network per entry.
sites.help.traffic.dnsbl.blacklistByType: Bans can target more than plain IP. You also get reverse DNS lists (for example .shodan.io), ASN lists (AS13335), user-agent substrings (curl/*) and URI substrings (/wp-admin).
sites.help.traffic.dnsbl.ja3Blacklist.us: Block traffic by TLS client fingerprint. Add MD5 hashes of known scanner JA3s (Shodan, Masscan) or malware C2. Use quick templates for preset sets. Sentinel activates signal_ja3_risk automatically when the list is non-empty.
sites.help.traffic.dnsbl.sources.label: External list sources
sites.help.traffic.dnsbl.sources.usage: URLs of files the WAF regularly reloads as updated IP, ASN, user-agent or URI lists. Handy for community-curated feeds.
sites.help.upstream.headers.title: Header forwarding: help
sites.help.upstream.headers.subtitle: Which headers the WAF adds or preserves when proxying to the upstream.
sites.help.upstream.headers.passHost.usa: Forwards the original client Host instead of the configured upstream host. Enable when the application differentiates sites by Host.
sites.help.upstream.headers.xff.label: X-Forwarded-For
sites.help.upstream.headers.xff.usage: Appends an X-Forwarded-For header carrying the real client IP and the proxy chain. The application must trust this header to see the user IP instead of the WAF IP.
sites.help.upstream.headers.xfp.label: X-Forwarded-Proto
sites.help.upstream.headers.xfp.usage: Forwards the client scheme (http or https). Required when the application builds absolute links or redirects, otherwise it assumes the client used HTTP.
sites.help.upstream.headers.xri.label: X-Real-IP
sites.help.upstream.headers.xri.usage: An alternative header carrying the client IP. Many applications and frameworks read it. Enable alongside X-Forwarded-For.
sites.easy.antibot.help.challenge: Primary bot challenge type. no disables the check, cookie sets an invisible cookie test, javascript asks the browser to run a script, captcha and recaptcha/hcaptcha/turnstile/mcaptcha turn on the matching external captcha provider. Pick the lightest mode that still works for you.
sites.easy.antibot.help.url: Internal path where the challenge page is served. /challenge by default. Change only if this URL conflicts with your application.
sites.easy.antibot.help.scanner: Hard-blocks obvious scanner traffic before any captcha is shown. Cuts botnet noise and reduces calls to the captcha provider.
sites.easy.antibot.help.recaptchaScore: Minimum score for score-based recaptcha. Range 0.0 to 1.0. Higher means stricter and more false rejections; 0.5 is the common default.
sites.easy.antibot.help.providerKeys: Captcha site key and secret. Fill in only for the active provider: recaptcha fields for recaptcha, hcaptcha for hcaptcha, and so on. Get the values from the provider dashboard.
sites.easy.antibot.help.exclusions: Paths and methods that bypass the bot challenge. Use for API, webhooks and any non-browser endpoints that cannot solve a captcha.
sites.easy.antibot.help.escalation: Enables a two-step challenge: after passing the first check, sensitive pages require a second, stricter one. Useful for login and admin areas.
sites.easy.antibot.help.escalationMode: Which challenge type to request for the second step when escalation is on. Usually stricter than the primary challenge.
sites.easy.antibot.help.challengeRules: Per-path overrides of the challenge, for example /login uses recaptcha while /api uses no. Useful for mixed API and browser traffic.
sites.easy.auth.help.enable: Turns the login wall on for this service. Leave it off only when the service is meant to be fully public.
sites.easy.auth.help.mode: Which credentials the service accepts. basic accepts HTTP Basic for humans. service_token accepts server tokens for machines. basic_or_token accepts both at once.
sites.easy.auth.help.order: Which check runs first: anti-bot or authentication. anti-bot first is safer against password brute force; authentication first is friendlier for trusted closed services.
sites.easy.auth.help.location: Authentication currently covers the whole service. The field is exposed for transparency and future per-path support.
sites.easy.auth.help.text: Greeting shown on the login page. Tell the user who is supposed to log in and what credentials to use.
sites.easy.auth.help.sessionTtl: Inactivity timeout in minutes for the session cookie. After this period the user must log in again.
sites.easy.auth.help.exclusions: Paths and methods that bypass the login page. Use for machine APIs, webhooks and ingest endpoints.
sites.easy.auth.help.users: Username and password list for humans. One row per user. Used by basic and basic_or_token modes.
sites.easy.auth.help.tokens: Static tokens for server-to-server integrations. Stored as a secret and sent in a header. Used by service_token and basic_or_token modes; rotate them regularly.
revisions.confirm.deleteOthers: Delete all revisions for {{service}} except the currently applied one? Revisions to delete: {{count}}.
revisions.toast.deleteOthersEmpty: There are no old revisions to delete for {{service}}.
dashboard.services.subtitle: Status and statistics of protected services.
sentinel.signal.credential_stuffing.expl: Repeated authentication failures on login endpoints suggest credential stuffing.
sentinel.signal.antibot_fail.label: Antibot Fail
sentinel.signal.antibot_fail.explanation: Source repeatedly failed antibot challenges indicating automated non-browser traffic.
sentinel.signal.bad_behavior.label: Bad Behavior
sentinel.signal.bad_behavior.explanation: Multiple bad-behavior zone hits indicate persistent policy-violating traffic patterns.
JA3 fingerprint risk and antibot failures are both present — high confidence automated threat.
dashboard.upstream_health.label: Upstream Health
sites.help.websocket.title: WebSocket Inspection
sites.help.websocket.subtitle: Control and filter WebSocket traffic
sites.help.websocket.useInspection.label: Enable Inspection
sites.help.websocket.useInspection.usage: Activates parsing and inspection of WS frame content. Requires WebSocket support enabled in upstream. Without this flag frames pass through unchecked.
sites.help.websocket.maxMessageBytes.usa: Limits the maximum size of a single WS frame in bytes. 0 means no limit. Recommended to set a limit to protect against large payload attacks.
sites.help.websocket.rateMsgPerSec.label: Max Messages/sec
sites.help.websocket.rateMsgPerSec.usage: Limits the rate of incoming WS messages from a single client. 0 disables the limit. Helps protect against WebSocket flood attacks.
sites.help.websocket.blockPatterns.label: Block Patterns
sites.help.websocket.blockPatterns.usage: List of regular expressions to block WS messages by content. If a frame matches any pattern the connection is terminated. Example: .*<script>.* to block XSS.
sites.help.virtualpatches.title: Virtual Patches
sites.help.virtualpatches.subtitle: Targeted request blocking without changing application code
sites.help.virtualpatches.overview.label: What is a virtual patch
sites.help.virtualpatches.overview.usage: A virtual patch blocks a specific attack vector at the WAF level without touching application code. Used for rapid mitigation before an official fix is released.
sites.help.virtualpatches.matchUri.label: URI Filter
sites.help.virtualpatches.matchUri.usage: Regex to match the request path. Empty means match any URI. Example: ^/admin/ to protect the admin panel.
sites.help.virtualpatches.matchMethod.us: HTTP method the patch applies to: GET, POST, PUT etc. If not set the patch applies to all methods.
sites.help.virtualpatches.matchBody.usag: Regex to check the request body. If content matches the request is blocked. Use to block known exploits by body signature.
sites.help.virtualpatches.action.label: Action
sites.help.virtualpatches.action.usage: Action on match: block — return 403, log — log only. In monitor mode patches always act as log regardless of this setting.
sites.help.virtualpatches.pattern.label: Pattern (regex)
sites.help.virtualpatches.pattern.usage: RE2 regular expression matched against the target field of the request. Example: ^/admin/ to block a URI path, or select.*from to catch SQL injection in the body. Applied to the field selected in Target.
sites.help.virtualpatches.target.label: Target
sites.help.virtualpatches.target.usage: Which request field the pattern is applied to: uri — request path, body — request body, header — request headers. One patch works with one target.
sites.help.upstreamMtls.title: Upstream mTLS (outgoing)
sites.help.upstreamMtls.subtitle: Mutual authentication when connecting to upstream
sites.help.upstreamMtls.enabled.label: Use client certificate
sites.help.upstreamMtls.enabled.usage: Enables sending a WAF client certificate when connecting to upstream. Use when upstream requires mutual certificate authentication.
sites.help.upstreamMtls.certRef.label: Certificate path
sites.help.upstreamMtls.certRef.usage: Path to the PEM client certificate file inside the container. Example: /etc/ssl/waf-client.crt
sites.help.upstreamMtls.keyRef.label: Key path
sites.help.upstreamMtls.keyRef.usage: Path to the private key file for the client certificate. Example: /etc/ssl/waf-client.key
sites.help.upstreamMtls.caRef.label: Upstream CA path
sites.help.upstreamMtls.caRef.usage: Path to the root CA certificate for verifying the upstream server certificate. Example: /etc/ssl/upstream-ca.crt
sites.easy.traffic.frame.blacklists.subt: IPs, domains, ASNs, User-Agents and URIs to block
sites.easy.traffic.frame.allowlists.subt: Addresses and networks that are not blocked
sites.help.front.main.title: Main service settings
sites.help.front.main.subtitle: Publishing, security and TLS parameters
sites.help.front.mtls.title: Mutual TLS (mTLS)
sites.help.front.mtls.subtitle: Client certificate verification
sites.help.traffic.allowlist.title: Allowlists: help
sites.help.traffic.allowlist.subtitle: Addresses and exceptions that always pass through.
sites.help.front.main.serverName.label: Hostname
sites.help.front.main.serverName.usage: Public domain the service answers on. Put the exact domain users will type, for example app.example.com. The domain must already point to this server so the WAF can validate it and issue certificates.
sites.help.front.main.serviceId.label: Service identifier
sites.help.front.main.serviceId.usage: Short internal name used in configs and logs. Latin lowercase with dashes, for example shop-prod. Leave empty to derive it from the hostname automatically.
sites.help.front.main.securityMode.label: Security mode
sites.help.front.main.securityMode.usage: Controls what the WAF does with suspicious requests. transparent passes everything and is only for debugging. monitor logs hits without blocking and is great for rule trialling. block actually blocks classified attacks and is the production default.
sites.help.front.main.serviceProfile.usa: Preset of rules tuned for a site type. strict for plain static sites, balanced for the common case, compat for legacy apps, api for REST and microservices, public-edge for high-traffic public portals. You can still adjust individual settings afterwards.
sites.help.front.main.caServer.label: Certificate authority
sites.help.front.main.caServer.usage: Where TLS certificates come from. letsencrypt and zerossl issue free certificates automatically. custom points to your own ACME server. import lets you upload an existing certificate manually.
sites.help.front.main.serviceEnabled.usa: Master switch for the service. With the flag off the WAF stops accepting traffic for this domain. Useful for a temporary outage without losing the configuration.
sites.help.front.main.adaptiveModel.usag: Enables the behavioural model that learns from real traffic and tightens defences on anomalies. Best for sites with steady, recurring audiences.
sites.help.front.main.autoLetsEncrypt.us: Order and renew Lets Encrypt certificates automatically. The domain must resolve to this server and ports 80 and 443 must be reachable from the public internet.
sites.help.front.main.letsEncryptStaging: Use the Lets Encrypt staging endpoint. Certificates will not be browser-trusted but you avoid hitting production issuance rate limits. Turn off before going live.
sites.help.front.main.wildcard.label: Wildcard certificate
sites.help.front.main.wildcard.usage: Order a wildcard certificate covering all subdomains. Requires DNS-01 support from the chosen authority and DNS control over the zone.
sites.help.front.main.tlsEnabled.label: TLS enabled
sites.help.front.main.tlsEnabled.usage: Accept HTTPS on this service. With the flag off the domain runs over plain HTTP. Always leave on in production.
sites.help.front.main.tlsSelfSigned.usag: Use a self-signed certificate. Browsers will show a warning. Only suitable for internal staging environments and debugging.
sites.help.front.main.certificateId.usag: Reference to a certificate that is already uploaded to the WAF. Only relevant when the authority is set to import; otherwise the value is filled in automatically.
sites.help.front.mtls.enabled.label: Require certificate
sites.help.front.mtls.enabled.usage: Require clients to present a certificate
sites.help.front.mtls.optional.label: Optional
sites.help.front.mtls.optional.usage: Do not block clients without certificate, only pass status
sites.help.front.mtls.passHeaders.label: Pass headers
sites.help.front.mtls.passHeaders.usage: Pass client certificate data in request headers
sites.help.front.mtls.verifyDepth.label: Verify depth
sites.help.front.mtls.clientCaRef.label: Client CA path
sites.help.front.mtls.clientCaRef.usage: Path to root CA for client certificate verification
sites.help.traffic.allowlist.overview.us: Allowlist passes addresses without WAF inspection
sites.help.traffic.allowlist.allowlistIp: IP addresses and subnets that always pass without WAF inspection.
IP addresses that bypass all blocking rules including blacklists and limits.
sites.help.traffic.allowlist.disabledHin: Allowlist disabled — all addresses are inspected by WAF
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.title: Blacklists: help
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.subtitle: Block by IP, ASN, User-Agent, URI, JA3 and DNSBL.
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.activate.us: Enables all blacklist checks. If disabled, lists are saved but not applied.
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.ip.label: IP blacklist
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.ip.usage: IP addresses and CIDR subnets to block.
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.rdns.label: rDNS blacklist
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.rdns.usage: Reverse DNS hostnames to block. Example: .shodan.io
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.asn.label: ASN blacklist
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.asn.usage: Autonomous systems to block. Format: AS12345.
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.userAgent.u: User-Agent patterns to block. Glob patterns supported.
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.uri.label: URI blacklist
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.uri.usage: URI paths to block. Glob patterns supported.
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.ja3.label: JA3 blacklist
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.ja3.usage: JA3 TLS fingerprints to block suspicious clients.
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.ipUrls.usag: Links to external files with IPs, rDNS, ASN, UA, URI and JA3 for automatic list updates.
sites.help.traffic.blacklist.dnsblActiva: Enables client IP lookup against external DNSBL providers listed below.
sites.help.traffic.allowlist.activate.us: Enables the IP allowlist. When active and populated, only listed addresses are allowed. IP and URI exceptions work independently of this flag.
sites.help.traffic.allowlist.exceptionsU: URI paths excluded from WAF inspection regardless of IP.
sites.easy.tab.httpheaders.title: HTTP & Headers
sites.easy.tab.httpheaders.subtitle: Methods, protocols and headers
